diagrams.title: FIFO vs LRU Page Replacement Comparison
diagrams.description: A side-by-side comparison of FIFO and LRU algorithms processing the same reference string (1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5) with 3 page frames. Shows how each algorithm handles page faults differently, with FIFO replacing the oldest page and LRU tracking usage history to make smarter replacements.
elements: 3 page frame boxes (left for FIFO, right for LRU)
Replacement arrows showing which page gets evicted
diagrams.purpose: Visual comparison to help students understand the fundamental difference between queue-based (FIFO) and usage-based (LRU) page replacement strategies and their impact on page fault rates

diagrams.title: Page Fault and Hit Mechanism
diagrams.description: A detailed visualization of the page replacement process showing memory frames, incoming page requests, and the decision flow. Demonstrates a page hit when the requested page exists in memory versus a page fault when it must be loaded, potentially causing an eviction.
elements: Page frame array (4-5 frames) showing loaded pages
Flow arrows for Hit vs Fault paths
diagrams.purpose: Explain the core mechanism of page hits and faults, helping students visualize what happens in memory during each memory access and why replacements become necessary

diagrams.title: Optimal Page Replacement Algorithm
diagrams.description: Demonstrates the OPT (Optimal) algorithm which looks ahead in the reference string to make perfect replacement decisions. Shows how it identifies which page will not be used for the longest time in the future, serving as a theoretical benchmark for comparing other algorithms.
elements: Reference string with future positions visible
Highlighted page to be replaced (optimal choice)
Backward look arrow showing algorithm reasoning
Comparison counter showing minimal faults achieved
diagrams.purpose: Illustrate the theoretical optimal algorithm to provide a performance upper-bound reference and help students understand why real-world algorithms aim to approximate this behavior
